text: Epictetus was born into slavery, likely in Hierapolis in the first century CE. His master, Epaphroditus, reportedly twisted his leg as a demonstration of power. According to Celsus, Epictetus said: 'You are breaking it.' When the leg snapped, he said simply: 'Did I not tell you?' The anecdote — possibly apocryphal — captures his philosophy in miniature. The body's destruction was beyond his control; his equanimity was not. His later students included the Roman emperor Marcus Aurelius, who read his Discourses at seventeen. That a freed slave could shape an emperor's inner life illustrates the dichotomy's radical democracy: rank commands nothing over the will.
question: What point does the passage make by noting that a freed slave shaped the inner life of an emperor?

options.text: That Stoicism was originally developed for the Roman ruling class
That Marcus Aurelius learned philosophy only from former slaves
That Epictetus eventually gained political influence through his teachings
That social rank has no authority over the will, illustrating the dichotomy's democratic nature
